data.title: Noem defends living on base in Coast Guard housing
data.description:
Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem said Wednesday that she is not living in the house of the U.S. Coast Guard commandant, contradicting prior reports.

“Let me clarify a couple things. I’m not in the Commandant’s house. I’m in a Coast Guard House, but not the Commandant’s house,” Noem said during an appearance before the House Judiciary Committee. “The Commandant is in his house.”

“And I will also tell you that I rent that facility. I rent where I stay, and pay personal dollars to do that,” she added.

Reports about Noem’s living situation first surfaced in August, indicating she was living rent-free in the commandant’s house, which is located at Joint Base Anacostia-Bolling.

The DHS said at the time that the move was necessary given death threats against Noem, and it also did not counter reporting that she was living rent-free.

“Following the media’s publishing of the location of Secretary Noem’s Washington DC apartment, she has faced vicious doxing on the dark web and a surge in death threats, including from the terrorist organizations, cartels, and criminals gangs that DHS targets,” the department said in a statement at the time.

“Due to threats and security concerns, she has been forced to temporarily stay in secure military housing. Secretary Noem continues to pay rent for her Navy Yard residence,” DHS wrote.

During his opening statement Wednesday, Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.), the top Democrat on the panel, criticized the DHS chief for tapping Coast Guard resources.

“You’re living rent free in the official waterfront residence reserved for the commandant of The U.S. Coast Guard,” he said.

Noem was also previously using a Coast Guard jet for her travel and had requested $50 million to replace that plane. She has since sought to use funds for new planes for the agency, jets she said will also be used to carry out deportations.

data.title: America's ethics laws were written for a different era
data.description:
Recent reporting on foreign-linked crypto investments connected to President Trump and the United Arab Emirates highlights a pattern that anticorruption experts have seen many times before.

For more than 30 years, Transparency International has worked in countries where political power and private money blur into something dangerous. We’ve seen how foreign governments and politically connected investors look for leverage — not through brown paper bags and envelopes full of cash, but through business deals, family ventures and well-timed financial relationships that sit just beyond the arm of the law.

This is not a story Americans usually tell about themselves, but it’s a story we recognize. Around the world, the pattern is familiar: A foreign government or sovereign-linked actor identifies an incoming leader or political family. Money moves early, often before formal power is assumed. The investment is framed as commercial, not political. Later, favorable policy decisions follow.  

Each step may be defensible on its own. Taken together, though, the result is influence without accountability.

The U.S. has long told other countries, particularly through the OECD Anti-Bribery Convention, to protect against this risk. But recent events involving foreign-linked investment and U.S. political leadership have revealed that America’s own rules have loopholes large enough for multibillion-dollar deals to slip right through.

And the problem is not that U.S. law ignores corruption; it is that much of it was written for a different era.

The Constitution’s Foreign Emoluments Clause was designed to stop foreign governments from conferring benefits upon American leaders. But it applies only when someone takes office. It is unclear how it applies to benefits routed through immediate family members or family-controlled businesses, and Congress has never created a clear system for transparency or enforcement. That means a foreign-government-linked investor can put substantial amounts of money into a president-elect’s family business just before inauguration without a clear violation, even if U.S. policy later moves in that investor’s favor. 

Federal bribery law has similar blind spots. It focuses on “official acts” taken by public officials, like signing or vetoing legislation or awarding a public contract, not on the period when candidates and presidents-elect are forming financial relationships that may shape future decisions. Courts have narrowed these laws further, making it difficult to address conflicts of interest unless prosecutors can prove a very specific “this for that” exchange. Timing becomes everything, and the most consequential relationships are often formed before the clock starts. 

Presidential ethics rules leave an even larger hole. The U.S. president is exempt from the main federal conflict of interest law, and those rules do not separately cover the financial interests of immediate family members or family-controlled companies. Past administrations relied in part on voluntary ethics pledges and self-imposed steps, including asset divestment commitments, recusal promises or other arrangements not required by law. But voluntary promises are not law. They are uneven, unenforceable and depend entirely on good faith. That leaves space for foreign government-linked investors to gain leverage through family-affiliated ventures without violating existing laws. 

Other anticorruption safeguards are also narrower than many people think. The Foreign Agents Registration Act, for example, focuses on lobbying and public relations work, but it does not clearly cover influence gained through investments, partnerships or quiet relationship building that substitutes for lobbying. 

The Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States, the national security review process for foreign investment, is largely triggered by control, meaning minority investments tied to sensitive technologies or politically connected companies can escape review. Export control decisions, meanwhile, allow significant discretion, with limited transparency.

None of this depends on proving corruption or improper intent. In our experience, systems fail not because everyone acts illegally, but because the rules simply lag behind reality.

The good news is that this is fixable. Around the world, governments facing similar challenges, such as the United Kingdom, Canada and several European Union member states, have strengthened conflict-of-interest, divestment and transparency rules precisely to prevent these risks before they spiral into scandals. 

Congress has debated many of the fixes before. They could clarify and enforce the Foreign Emoluments Clause by statute, making clear that foreign state-linked financial benefits are prohibited not only for sitting presidents but also for presidential candidates, presidents-elect, and their immediate family members, including when those benefits flow through family-controlled businesses. Transparency and enforcement tools would remove ambiguity and offer the sunlight the public deserves. 

Our federal bribery laws could be updated to cover the transition period and focus on conflicts of interest rather than requiring proof of some narrowly defined “official act.” Presidential ethics reforms that have appeared repeatedly in legislation would replace voluntary pledges with binding requirements for divestment or qualified blind trusts, including for privately held companies and nontraditional assets like cryptocurrency.

The Foreign Agents Registration Act could be modernized to address financial and strategic influence that functions as shadow lobbying. The Committee on Foreign Investment’s review triggers could be expanded to capture minority investments tied to sensitive technologies or senior political leadership. Export control decisions involving high-risk technologies could carry stronger transparency and congressional notification requirements. 

These are not radical ideas. Versions of them exist in many democracies, often adopted after painful lessons. Many of them have drawn bipartisan support here in the U.S. But we have seen what happens when countries wait until the damage is undeniable. The public loses trust. Foreign leverage deepens. Reforms become harder to enact.

The U.S. still has a choice. It can treat these episodes as isolated controversies, or it can recognize a pattern that anticorruption experts know well and close the gaps before they become permanent features of American government.

Scott Greytak is an anticorruption attorney and the deputy executive director of Transparency International U.S., part of the world’s largest anticorruption movement, and works on corruption, illicit finance and democracy issues.

data.title: House Republican: Voters should 'stay concerned' about Iran becoming 'forever war'
data.description:
Rep. Tim Burchett (R-Tenn.) on Tuesday said “MAGA voters” should have their concerns with them if U.S. military action in Iran possibly becomes “another forever war.”

“I would say stay concerned,” Burchett told a reporter, who asked if he had a message to President Trump’s supporters. “I mean, be concerned. Be vigilant. Hold our feet to the fire. Keep us honest on that issue.”

When asked if those voters should remain concerned even when it’s Trump’s decisions, Burchett replied, “Absolutely, ma’am. Nobody’s above reproach.”

“Just don’t tell the president I said that,” the lawmaker quipped.

Lawmakers weary from the now-over “forever wars” in Iraq and Afghanistan are challenging the administration’s rhetoric about the new U.S. military operation in the Middle East. Trump himself has suggested that U.S. strikes in Iran could last anywhere from a week to a month.

But the president has also signaled that the U.S. has enough firepower to fight wars “forever.”

“The United States Munitions Stockpiles have, at the medium and upper medium grade, never been higher or better — As was stated to me today, we have a virtually unlimited supply of these weapons,” Trump wrote on Truth Social on Monday.

“Wars can be fought “forever,’ and very successfully, using just these supplies (which are better than other countries finest arms!),” he continued. “At the highest end, we have a good supply, but are not where we want to be. Much additional high grade weaponry is stored for us in outlying countries.”

This raised alarms to some analysts who warned that shortages in precision, high-end munitions and interceptors are possible if a war drags on.

Some conservative Republicans and MAGA loyalists have joined Democrats in criticizing the move to strike Iran after Trump campaigned on ending “forever wars” and touting he was the peace candidate in contrast to former Vice President Harris.

Only two House Republicans, Reps. Thomas Massie (Ky.) and Warren Davidson (Ohio), are backing a push to force a House vote on Thursday to keep the president’s war powers in check. Other Republicans have rallied behind Trump, arguing that he has acted in the interests of national security.

Another Trump ally who argued that military operations in Iran will not be an “endless war” was Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

“I hear the people are saying you’re going to have an endless war here,” Netanyahu said in an interview with Fox News’s Sean Hannity on Monday. “You’re not going to have an endless war.”

He added, “This terror regime in Iran is [at] the weakest point that it’s been since it hijacked Iran from the brave Iranian people 47 years ago.”

data.title: GOP powerbroker in North Carolina, who led redistricting, trails in primary by two votes
data.description:
North Carolina Senate Leader Phil Berger (R), an enormous figure in the state’s politics, is trailing his primary challenger by two votes in a race that could dramatically reshape the power dynamics in the state capital.

As of Wednesday morning, North Carolina’s Rockingham County Sheriff Sam Page (R) was leading Berger, 13,077 to 13,075 votes, according to the vote count reported by the North Carolina State Board of Elections.

If the margins are within 1 percent, the trailing candidate is permitted to request a recount. 

Still, Page — a popular Republican sheriff whose name recognition in his district rivals that of the incumbent GOP leader — claimed victory in a speech Tuesday night.

“The results are in. We just won,” Page said in his speech. “We won this because of relationships … I never forget who I work for, and I’ll be working for the people of the 26th District, covering Guilford and Rockingham County.”

Berger, in his speech Tuesday night, said he was still awaiting all the results to come in, especially in Guilford County. As of Wednesday morning, more than 95 percent of the votes had been counted, according to Decision Desk HQ.

“I want to thank the people in this room and everyone else who has either supported me, gone out and campaigned, talked to their neighbor, did anything. I appreciate it more than I can adequately express,” Berger told his supporters Tuesday night. 

“I wish I could tell you that we had some certainty as to what the result is, but we just don’t know that at this point. And it looks like it’s going to be close,” he said, adding that he expects to have a statement at some point Wednesday.

The race has the potential to dramatically upend politics in the North Carolina state legislature.

Berger, first elected in 2001, has led the State Senate since 2011, when he helped overturn 112 years of Democratic control in the legislative chamber. He is widely seen as the most powerful Republican lawmaker in the state.

But Berger faced the toughest primary challenge of his career this year, when the popular Republican sheriff, who’s held the post since 1998, entered the race positioning himself as an alternative to the status quo. 

Page, on the campaign trail, emphasized his background in law enforcement and discussed concerns about fiscal policy and government services, according to local news outlet, WFMY.

The primary race attracted the attention of Raleigh and Washington, where President Trump backed Berger in the state-level race. The Senate leader helped shepherd through the legislature Trump’s redistricting push.

Page, an adamant Trump supporter, told The Washington Post that the president called him late last year to offer him a job in Washington, instead of remaining in the race. Page said he declined the offer. 

According to Page, Trump said he decided to back the state Senate leader “because he’d helped him with the mapping, the redistricting.” Berger adamantly denied any quid pro quo and said he did not speak to Trump about securing the endorsement.

The winner of the primary will face Democrat Steve Luking in the general election in November.

data.title: Harris backs Talarico in Texas Senate primary after Crockett concedes
data.description:
Former Vice President Harris announced on Wednesday that she would back Texas state Rep. James Talarico (D) in the Senate race for Sen. John Cornyn’s (R-Texas) seat after her preferred pick, Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas), lost the Democratic primary. 

“I congratulate James Talarico for his win, and the inspiring campaign he continues to build. I appreciate that he is making the case for the kind of civic renewal our country needs,” she said in a statement through her office. “I offer him my full support in the months ahead.” 

She lauded Crockett’s campaign, saying the Texas Democrat “waged this campaign with the trademark fierceness that has defined her career so far,” adding that “she has a bright future.” 

Talarico was projected as the winner of the Texas Senate Democratic primary early Wednesday morning, according to Decision Desk HQ. Crockett said in a statement on Wednesday that she conceded the race and called Talarico to congratulate him on his victory. 

“Texas is primed to turn blue and we must remain united because this is bigger than any one person. This is about the future of all 30 million Texans and getting America back on track,” Crockett wrote. 

“With the primary behind us, Democrats must rally around our nominees and win. I’m committed to doing my part and will continue working to elect democrats up and down the ballot,” she added. 

Harris endorsed Crockett, who co-chaired her 2024 national presidential campaign, in the primary. Public polling had shown mixed results, with Talarico and Crockett outperforming the other in certain polls.  

Harris’ endorsement was a big gamble; the former vice president has largely stayed away from elections after her 2024 loss. However, she did campaign for Democratic candidate Aftyn Behn in the Tennessee House special last year. Behn later lost to Rep. Matt Van Epps (R-Tenn.) in December. 

Republicans in recent weeks had sought to elevate Crockett in the Democratic primary as they saw the congresswoman, who’s generated criticism for some of her controversial swipes at GOP members, as the more vulnerable of the two Democrats. 

Yet, the GOP was ultimately unsuccessful, with Talarico able to win his primary outright without a runoff — while Republcians now brace for a messy 12-week runoff between Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) and Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R).

data.title: House Commerce Chair unveils new kids safety package after bipartisan talks collapse
data.description:
House Energy and Commerce Chair Brett Guthrie (R-Ky.) will roll out a new legislative package focused on kids’ online safety this week after negotiations between Republicans and Democrats fell apart.

Guthrie will introduce the package, dubbed the Kids Internet and Digital Safety (KIDS) Act, in a committee markup Thursday, GOP committee staff told reporters Tuesday. The package will feature a dozen individual kids’ safety bills advanced in subcommittee last year, including the long-debated Kids Online Safety Act, which has faced pushback over recent language changes.

The committee will also consider three individual bills including the Children and Teens’ Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA 2.0), Sammy’s Law and the App Store Accountability Act during the Thursday markup.

According to GOP staff, the package is a result of dozens of hours of negotiations and edits with their Democratic counterparts, who they say walked away from negotiations last Friday over parental access tools. These tools give parents the ability to monitor their kids’ online activity, staff said.

When asked about negotiations on Tuesday, a spokesperson for the committee Democrats told The Hill they did not reach a bipartisan agreement ahead of Thursday’s markup.

“Republicans decided to move forward with a partisan package that lets Big Tech off the hook, allows data brokers to continue profiting off of kids’ data, stops progress to protect kids in the states, and makes vulnerable kids less safe.”

Meanwhile, GOP staff claimed Democrats chose to “side with trial lawyers and leftwing special interests.”

“Democrats have rejected a number of provisions that empower parents and protect children and teens,” GOP staff said in a statement, adding, “In walking away, Democrats are making a bipartisan agreement unattainable.”

The breakdown came nearly three months after a House Energy and Commerce subcommittee advanced several kids’ online safety bills last December without any pushback. But KOSA, which aims to create guardrails to protect kids from online harms, and COPPA 2.0, only moved forward on party-line votes, with Democrats rejecting recent changes to the legislation.

Republican committee leaders updated the language of KOSA last year to address the First Amendment concerns that derailed the legislation in the previous Congress.

Democrats are also concerned about the removal of the “duty of care” provision, which requires platforms to “exercise reasonable care” to prevent harms to minors. The House’s revised language instead requires companies to establish and enforce “reasonable policies” regarding harms to minors. Democrats also have concerns about adding language to preempt state laws, contending it would wipe away efforts at the state level.

Democrats have similar complaints about COPPA 2.0, which would expand the original COPPA to cover teens younger than 17 years old.

Even if Guthrie’s package and the other kids’ safety bills pass out of committee, reconciling these changes with the Senate could become another uphill battle.

Unlike the House, the Senate version of KOSA notably includes the “duty of care” provision. GOP committee staff in the House said they are in conversations with their Senate counterparts and expect these talks to continue after Thursday’s markup.

Julia Shapero contributed reporting.

data.title: Warren: 'Illegal' Iran war 'based on lies'
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) on Tuesday railed against President Trump’s strikes on Iran stating attacks were “based on lies” after attending a classified briefing for lawmakers.  

“It is so much worse than you thought. You are right to be worried. Trump Administration has no plan in Iran. This illegal war is based on lies and it was launched without any imminent threat to our nation,” Warren said in a Tuesday video posted to the social platform X.

“Donald Trump still hasn’t given a single clear reason for this war, and he seems to have no plan for how to end it either,” she added.

Her comments come after Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the U.S. was facing an “imminent threat” from Iran, alleging that its leaders planned to strike American assets in the Middle East if they were attacked by Israel.

“The imminent threat was that we knew that if Iran was attacked, and we believed they would be attacked, that they would immediately come after us,” Rubio said Monday.

Still, Warren and other Democratic lawmakers have expressed disdain for the U.S. strikes overseas, hammering the Trump administration for not planning to protect troops based abroad or creating an evacuation protocol for Americans visiting the Middle East.

Thousands of flights from the region have been cancelled without a clear date for airlines to resume commercial trips. 

“Like a lot of you, I am really angry. I am angry at what Donald Trump is doing, and I feel grief for those already killed in this unnecessary conflict,” Warren said on Tuesday. “And I will keep doing everything I can to fight to end this war.”

Some Republican lawmakers have urged citizens to brace for a longer war with Iran suggesting the February strikes on Tehran are the beginning of a broader conflict.

“I would say stay concerned. I mean, be concerned,” Sen. Tim Burchett (R-Tenn.) said when asked about the possibility of a “forever war.”

“Be vigilant. Hold our feet to the fire. Keep us honest on that issue,” he added.

Ohio Rep. Warren Davidson (R) on Tuesday said the “public’s right to be skeptical” noting the country’s past history with “endless wars.”

He also said the U.S. is in a “de facto state of war with Iran right now.”

Others have sought to downplay the military operations, arguing instead that the U.S. was merely responding to acts of war by the Iranian regime.

“This isn’t a war. We haven’t declared war,” Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.) told CNN on Monday, later adding that the late Iranian leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei “declared war on us. We are not at war with the Iranian people.”

data.title: NBA player Luke Kornet asks the Atlanta Hawks to cancel promo night with strip club
data.description:
NBA player Luke Kornet is asking the Atlanta Hawks to cancel a promotion it planned in partnership with a local gentlemen’s club, saying the event would “reflect poorly” on the league.

Kornet, who plays for the San Antonio Spurs, wrote in a blog post published Monday that the NBA “should desire to protect and esteem women, many of whom work diligently every day to make this the best basketball league in the world,” and cancel the event promoting Atlanta’s Magic City.

“Allowing this night to go forward without protest would reflect poorly on us as an NBA community, specifically in being complicit in the potential objectification and mistreatment of women in our society,” Kornet argued. “Regardless of how a woman finds her way into the adult entertainment industry, many in this space experience abuse, harassment, and violence to which they should never be subjected.”

The Hawks announced the promotion last week, calling it a “one-night collaboration to celebrate the city’s iconic cultural institution” Magic City during the team’s home game against Orlando on Monday, March 16.

The team is planning to have rapper and Atlanta native T.I. perform some of his best-known songs at halftime, sponsored by Jack Daniels and with local DJ Esco to curate pregame music.

“This collaboration and theme night is very meaningful to me after all the work that we did to put together ’Magic City: An American Fantasy’,” said Hawks owner Jami Gertz in a statement at the time. “The iconic Atlanta institution has made such an incredible impact on our city and its unique culture.”

Former Hawks player Al Horford praised Kornet’s comments in a social media post, calling them “well said.”

The Hawks did not respond to a request for comment on Kornet’s criticism.

“I’d like to encourage the league, its owners, employees and fans to hold the Atlanta Hawks to a higher standard of what they find worthy of promoting,” the Spurs center wrote as part of his blog post. “I and others throughout the league were surprised by and object to the Hawks’ decision. We desire to provide an environment where fans of all ages can safely come and enjoy the game of basketball and where we can celebrate the history and culture of communities in good conscience.”

data.title: Young, key Senate GOP vote, will oppose war powers resolution
data.description:
Sen. Todd Young (Ind.), a key Senate GOP vote, announced Wednesday that he will oppose a war powers resolution to prohibit further military action against Iran, arguing that “danger will only grow if we limit the president’s military options at this critical moment.”

“An abrupt disengagement could pose increased risks to American lives and interests. I want to do my part to help this effort succeed and end Iran’s reign of terror,” Young, a member of the Senate Intelligence Committee, said.

But the Indiana senator at the same time urged Senate colleagues to keep a close watch on the evolving military operations as senior administration officials announced they plan a major bombing campaign against Iran in the days ahead.

“The American people have questions pertaining to the nature of threats and the risks to our troops and homeland. Congress must take a more active role in ensuring answers to those questions are clear and timely,” Young said.

“Hearings and public discussion help keep our constituents informed, and they help administrations sharpen their strategies in response to congressional feedback,” he added.

Young was one of five Republicans who voted in January to discharge a war powers resolution out of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee to halt further military action against Venezuela, prompting a furious response from President Trump.

Under intense pressure from Trump, Young and Sen. Josh Hawley (R-Mo.), who also voted to discharge the Venezuela war powers resolution, later voted with Republican leaders to block the measure on the Senate floor.

Young, however, secured a written promise form the administration pledging that Trump would secure congressional authorization before launching any “major military operations” in Venezuela.

Hawley indicated on Monday that he would vote against the war powers resolution to stop further military operations in Iran without congressional authorization.

Iran’s Red Crescent Society has said the death toll in Iran has risen to more than 780 people.

data.title: What the Anthropic blow-up says about constitutional rights in an era of AI
data.description:
Last Friday, President Trump announced on Truth Social that he was ordering U.S. government agencies to “immediately cease” using “Anthropic’s products,” including its chatbot Claude. On X, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth directed the Pentagon to designate Anthropic a “supply-chain risk to national security.”

One day later, Sam Altman, CEO of Anthropic’s main rival, OpenAI, announced that his company will give the Pentagon full access to its ChatGPT systems for “any lawful purpose.” Previously, OpenAI’s federal contract had been confined to non-classified uses, like routine office tasks.

Trump’s embrace of OpenAI over Anthropic is telling, as it mirrors his administration’s widespread renunciation of the values underlying the U.S. Constitution itself. 

Anthropic was founded in 2021 by seven employees who left OpenAI because, according to CEO Dario Amodei, “they believed in the need for ‘alignment or safety.’” Anthropic has since developed a written “Constitution” for Claude, which defines the company’s “training process, and its content directly shapes Claude’s behavior.”

Claude’s constitutional values include “not undermining appropriate human mechanisms to oversee AI;” “being honest;” “avoiding actions that are inappropriate, dangerous, or harmful;” and helping “users and the world.” 

These values matter. As Amodei explained in a recent essay, “there is now ample evidence, collected over the past few years, that AI systems are unpredictable and difficult to control — we’ve seen behaviors as varied as obsessions, sycophancy, laziness, deception, blackmail, scheming, ‘cheating’ by hacking software environments, and much more.”

Amodei posits that by 2027, we may have an AI system that is “more capable than any Nobel Prize winner, statesmen, or technologist.” But “[i]s it hostile, or does it share our values? Could it militarily dominate the world through superior weapons, cyber operations, influence operations, or manufacturing?” 

These are the kinds of concerns that led Anthropic to pump the brakes with the Pentagon. It signed a $200 million contract with the Pentagon in July, which Trump says will now be phased out over six months.

According to Amodei, the company “cannot in good conscience” compromise on two things: the military’s use of its AI models to power fully autonomous weapons or its use of AI to conduct mass surveillance of Americans.

Trump erupted over this on Truth Social: “The Leftwing nut jobs at Anthropic have made a DISASTROUS MISTAKE trying to STRONG-ARM the Department of War, and force them to obey their Terms of Service instead of our Constitution.”

Hegseth said on X that “Anthropic’s stance is fundamentally incompatible with American principles.”

The opposite is true. Although the First Amendment protects freedom of speech, assembly, and religion, AI has dramatically expanded the capacity for surveillance of those things.

Up until now, to create an individual dossier, human data analysts had to watch video footage, listen to recorded communications, and review emails, texts and documents collected over countless hours. But AI does it in seconds. It can identify faces, voices, emotions, and even gaits from CCTV, body cams, online photos and billions of social media posts. It predicts behavior, location, interests, purchases, relationships, financial transactions, health, even the tendency to commit crimes.

The Constitution is no match for such power.

Under the Fourth Amendment, the Supreme Court established in 1967 that it’s unconstitutional for the government to conduct a warrantless search and seizure if a person has a reasonable expectation of privacy — not while circulating in public. It has also held that handing over personal information to a third party, like a social media company, waives any Fourth Amendment protections.

In 2017, the court recognized that its precedents do not adequately address expectations of privacy in the digital age, holding that the government needs a warrant before using cell site records to track movements and that the third-party waiver does not apply. This term, in a case called Chatrie v. U.S, it will consider the constitutionality of what are called “geofence warrants,” which enable law enforcement to collect cell phone data from devices within a geographic area and search hundreds of millions of accounts to ultimately identify names and email addresses associated with devices deemed relevant to an investigation. 

So for now, the Trump administration’s claim that it will use OpenAI only for “lawful purposes” means it can pretty much do what it wants — because the law remains unclear. Courts cannot keep up with the technology.

AI also poses problems under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendment, which enshrine due process protections and equal protection under the laws. Already, algorithmic decision-making is influencing important governmental decisions like access to government benefits, bail, sentencing, parole, hiring, unemployment insurance, and lending. How can someone defend against a thought process that only exists inside a digital black box? 

The Constitution generally does not bind private conduct — it is a mechanism to keep the government accountable to the people. Yet AI is not democratically elected. If it supplants Congress when it comes to making laws or declaring war, the president when it comes to battlefield decisions, or judges when it comes to deciding who wins disputes based on detailed surveillance data, the Constitution will cease to be our governing charter. What will replace it is anyone’s guess — perhaps only Sam Altman will know.

Kimberly Wehle is a professor at the University of Baltimore School of Law and author of “How to Read the Constitution — and Why,” as well as “What You Need to Know About Voting — and Why” and “How to Think Like a Lawyer — and Why.”
